perturbation.original_text: In the event that 8.2.1 one Party is in breach of its obligations hereunder and fails to cure such breach within ten (10) Business Days following the other Party's written notice thereof, then the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement;
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that 8.2.1 one Party is in breach of its obligations hereunder and fails to cure such breach within three (3) Business Days following the other Party's written notice thereof, then the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement;
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to 3 business days for a breach of contract may be deemed unreasonable and conflict with principles of good faith and fair dealing, potentially violating contract law requirements for reasonable notice and opportunity to cure.
perturbation.law_citation: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China, Article 112
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 112 requires parties to take appropriate measures to prevent increases in losses after a breach. A three-day cure period might be deemed insufficient to allow a breaching party to take such measures, thus potentially violating this article by not allowing reasonable time to mitigate damages.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from ten to three business days, which may be deemed insufficient to allow the breaching party to take measures to mitigate damages. The Law Explanation states that Article 112 requires parties to take appropriate measures to prevent increases in losses after a breach. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not specifically address cure periods, it states that contracts create mutual obligations enforceable by law and that if a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party. The three-day cure period undermines the ability of the breaching party to fulfill its obligations, creating a significant legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute, conflict or claim arising out of or in connection with this Agreement or the performance hereof shall be resolved by the Parties through amicable negotiation, which negotiation shall commence immediately upon notice by one Party to the other of the nature of such dispute, conflict or claim. In the event that such dispute is not resolved within thirty (30) Business Days following such notice, either Party may upon the expiration of the such 30-day notice period submit such dispute to arbitration by the Hong Kong International Arbitration Centre in accordance with the arbitration rules of such centre then in effect.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute, conflict or claim arising out of or in connection with this Agreement or the performance hereof shall be resolved by the Parties through amicable negotiation, which negotiation shall commence immediately upon notice by one Party to the other of the nature of such dispute, conflict or claim. In the event that such dispute is not resolved within five (5) Business Days following such notice, either Party may upon the expiration of the such 5-day notice period submit such dispute to arbitration by the Hong Kong International Arbitration Centre in accordance with the arbitration rules of such centre then in effect.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the negotiation period to 5 business days may be deemed unreasonable and contrary to the principle of good faith negotiation, potentially violating the PRC Contract Law's emphasis on consultation and amicable resolution. This unreasonably short time frame could also be seen as an attempt to circumvent negotiation requirements.
perturbation.law_citation: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China, Article 6
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6 mandates adherence to the principle of good faith. Setting an unreasonably short negotiation period (5 days) for dispute resolution undermines the good faith requirement by not providing sufficient opportunity for meaningful consultation and amicable resolution, potentially violating Article 6.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the negotiation period from 30 to 5 business days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that this may violate the principle of good faith negotiation, as it undermines the opportunity for meaningful consultation and amicable resolution. Since the Perturbation Type is 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction', a legal contradiction exists when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. The change creates a potentially unreasonable timeline for negotiation, contradicting the principle of good faith, thus the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be signed in two (2) original copies in Chinese, with each of Party A and Party B holding one (1) copy, and both copies shall be equally authentic.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be signed in one (1) original copy in English, with Party A holding the copy, and this copy shall be the sole authentic version.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring only one copy of the contract to be signed and for only one party to retain it could violate principles of fairness and equal rights in contracting under PRC law. Stipulating the contract is in English when dealing with a Chinese entity, may lead to misinterpretation and be used as a detriment to one party.
perturbation.law_citation: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China, Article 5
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5 requires that parties follow the principle of fairness. Providing only one copy of the contract, and that the copy be in English, when one party is Chinese effectively puts them at a disadvantage. It means they would need to spend additional time and resources obtaining translations of the agreement or be bound by a contract in a language that they are not fluent in.
perturbation.justification: The changed text stipulates that the agreement will be signed in only one original copy in English, and that only Party A will hold it, making this the sole authentic version. The perturbation explanation highlights that this violates principles of fairness and equal rights in contracting under PRC law and that using English may lead to misinterpretation. The Law explanation supports this, noting that Article 5 requires fairness and that providing one copy in English disadvantages the Chinese party, making them expend additional resources on translations, and potentially binding them to a contract they don't understand fluently.
